headline: Wheat Dumps 14¢, Milk Explodes 15% on Supply Squeeze
subheadline: Illinois takes an inch of rain this week while cattle, feeders rally on tight supplies.
teaser: Rain locks up Illinois fields, wheat craters, milk soars on tight supply.

one_number.why: That's enough to delay burndown and push back your planting window if fields are already saturated.

field_call.label: STAY OUT — ILLINOIS
field_call.detail: Central IL gets an inch through Saturday with heaviest rain Thursday. Iowa, SD dry until Friday.

sections.title: Grains & Oilseeds
bullets: Corn $4.45¼, down 2¾¢. Dec '26 at $4.69¼. The 24¢ carry signals comfortable supplies — no urgency to sell old crop yet.
Beans $11.61¾, down 8½¢. Meal at $312.50, oil 62.68¢. Crush margins tightening as meal weakens faster than oil.
Wheat $5.76¼, down 14½¢ in a 2.5% dump. Export demand soft, dollar strength making U.S. wheat less competitive globally.
USDA crush report: 228 million bushels of beans crushed in January, ethanol burned 505 million bushels of corn.

sections.title: Livestock & Dairy
bullets: Live cattle $233.65, up $1.42. Feeders $353.70, up $2.73. Tight supplies keeping pressure under both — packers need cattle.
Hogs $95.58, essentially flat. Market waiting for spring demand to show up.
Class III milk exploded $2.29 to $17.21 — a 15% one-day jump on supply tightness. Lock in Q2 milk if you haven't.

sections.title: Weather & Fieldwork
bullets: Central Illinois takes 1.02 inches through Saturday, heaviest Thursday (0.40in). Burndown applications off the table until next week.
Iowa, Nebraska, South Dakota stay mostly dry until Friday, then 0.35-0.45 inches weekend rain closes the window.
Temps: 40s-50s across the Belt today, warming to upper 60s by Friday before rain cools things back down.
Bottom line: Iowa/Nebraska guys have Wednesday-Thursday to spray. Illinois is done for the week.

sections.title: Energy & Macro
bullets: Crude $71.03, down 28¢. Diesel pressure eases slightly but spring fill-up still expensive. Anhydrous costs holding firm.
Dollar at 98.51, up 66 ticks. Stronger dollar makes U.S. corn and beans pricier for foreign buyers — headwind for exports.
Natgas up 9¢ to $2.98. Fertilizer plants watching this closely — urea already spiking on Mideast supply fears per Farm Progress.

sections.title: Policy & Trade
bullets: USDA FAS chief calls U.S.-China ag trade 'complicated but essential' — that's code for 'don't expect miracles but we need their billion consumers.'
Missouri FAPRI report: wider E15 adoption would boost corn prices but pressure soybean values as acreage shifts. Trade-off worth watching.
Dicamba approved for 2026-2027 use per EPA, but legal challenges continue. Don't assume it's permanent — have Plan B for resistant weeds.

watch_list.label: Today
watch_list.item: Illinois fields — if you're muddy now, you're staying muddy through weekend.

watch_list.label: This Week
watch_list.item: Soybean crush margins — meal weakness is starting to squeeze processor economics.

watch_list.label: Horizon
watch_list.item: March 31 USDA prospective plantings report. Corn vs bean acres will move markets.

watch_list.label: Risk
watch_list.item: Dollar strength kills export demand. Every tick higher makes Brazilian beans cheaper than ours.

watch_list.label: Opportunity
watch_list.item: Lock Q2 milk now while Class III is at $17+. That rally won't last forever.

the_more_you_know.title: 228 Million Bushels Crushed
the_more_you_know.body: That's how many soybeans got turned into meal and oil in January alone. At 60 pounds per bushel, that's 13.7 billion pounds of beans running through U.S. crushers in one month — roughly 445 million pounds per day.

daily_quote.text: The farmer has to be an optimist or he wouldn't still be a farmer.

source_summary: Data: CME Group futures settlements, NWS forecasts (5 Corn Belt stations), USDA NASS reports, ag news aggregation from Farm Journal, Farm Progress, Brownfield, AgDaily, Agweek, Morning Ag Clips.
